**[A letter, penned with a quill and ink, the paper slightly frayed at the edges, bearing the faint scent of sea brine and aged parchment]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a wounded beast and the waves crash against the cliffs as if they would swallow the lighthouse whole.**  
**I do not know if I shall live to see dawn, but if I do, I must tell you this: I have not forgotten you, and I have not ceased to pray for your soul.**

**Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have I borne the weight of your absence.**  
**I have watched the sea from this tower, and I have seen the stars, but never the face of my child.**  
**You were once my light, Eleanor, and now I am left in darkness.**

**I know not what drove you away, nor do I seek to know.**  
**You left with a man who was not your husband, and with a child you did not name.**  
**You left me with the burden of your silence, and I have carried it like a stone upon my chest.**

**But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of my years pressing down upon me.**  
**I have kept this lighthouse for over thirty years, and I have never faltered in my

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now in the dim light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside. The sea is restless, and I fear it may take more than just the night from me. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see morning.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not spoken to you in that time. I have not written, either. I have not known what to say. You were a child once, and I was a father. But time, like the tide, has taken what it will, and I have not been able to stop it.

I do not know if you still remember the nights we spent by the fire, you curled in your blanket, listening to the waves and the stories I told you of sailors lost to the sea. I do not know if you still remember the scent of the sea, or the sound of the lighthouse bell. I do not know if you remember me at all.

I have always been a man of few words, Eleanor. I have always been a man of the sea. But I have never been a man of the heart. And yet, as this storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of my silence more than ever.

You were taken from me, not by death, but by choice. I do not blame you. I do not know if I could have stopped you. You were always the brave one,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast unleashed upon the cliffs, and the sea roiling as if it too feels the weight of the night. I have not spoken to you in five years, not a word, not a letter, not even a glance from across the harbor. And yet, tonight, as the storm gathers, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, to the past, to the silence that has grown between us.

You were not the one who left, Eliza. I know that now. I have spent these years standing at the edge of this lighthouse, watching the world from the top of the tower, and I have come to understand that I was the one who turned away. I was the one who let the world slip through my fingers, and in doing so, I let you slip away too.

I know you think me cruel, that I abandoned you when you needed me most. But I was not cruel. I was broken. The weight of this lighthouse, the burden of keeping the light burning through every storm, every night, every hour—it has shaped me, hardened me. I did not leave you; I was left behind. And I have not been the same since.

I have seen the sea from every angle, the way it devours ships, the way it whispers secrets to those who will listen. I have watched the stars for years, their light a beacon

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it seeks to devour the very stones of this cliff. I fear the night may claim me before dawn, but I must speak, for I have not yet told you the truth of my days.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not brought the comfort of your presence. I have kept the lighthouse lit through every storm, every night, every moment of solitude. Yet, I have not been able to find the strength to reach out to you, to beg your forgiveness or to ask for your return.

You were but a child when you ran from me, and I did not understand the pain you carried. I thought I could shield you from the world's cruelty, but I was blind to the storm within your soul. I have spent these years in silence, not out of spite, but because I feared my words would do more harm than good.

Tonight, as the tempest gathers, I find myself haunted by memories of you. I remember the way you used to run through the garden, your laughter like the wind, and the way your eyes would light up at the sight of the sea. But now, I see the shadow that has grown in their place, the sorrow that has taken root where once there was joy.

I do not know if I shall

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast at the door, and the sea roars as though it seeks to reclaim what it once held. The lantern is lit, as it has been for thirty years, and though the light may flicker, it shall not go out. Not while I still draw breath.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, this life, and the man I am. I have not spoken your name in five years, nor have I dared to think of you. Yet now, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself writing, not to beg for forgiveness, but to say what I have long kept locked behind my heart.

You were not the only one who changed, Eleanor. You were not the only one who left. I was not the man I was when you were a child. I was not the father who held you in his arms when you were sick, who read you stories by the firelight, who promised you that no matter what, the lighthouse would always be there.

I did not know, when you left, that you would never return. I did not know that the silence between us would grow so thick it would choke the air. I did not know that the sea, which once sang lullabies to you, would one day swallow your voice.

But I do know this: I have not forgotten the way you looked when

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse. The sea roars in protest, its waves crashing with a fury that seems to echo the storm of my own heart. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear the silence may never be broken. Yet, as the tempest gathers strength, I feel compelled to reach out—to you, my daughter, my only child.

Five years is a long time, Eleanor. Five years since you left, not with a parting word, but with a silent dismissal, as if I had been a stranger to you. I have tried to forget, to move on, to let time mend what words could not. But the sea never forgets, and neither does the heart.

You were only twelve when you left. A child, yes, but with a spirit as fierce as the storm that now threatens to claim us all. I remember the day you walked out of this house, your small figure disappearing into the mist, your voice barely audible over the wind. You said you would return when you were older, when you could make your own way in the world. And I, in my foolish pride, allowed you to go. I thought I was protecting you. I thought I was giving you a chance. But I was wrong.

You have never written to me, and I have never written to you. The letters

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. I write to you now, not with the hope of mending what has been broken between us, but with the weight of the storm that now threatens to swallow this lighthouse—and perhaps my life—whole.

Five years have passed since the last time I saw your face. Time has been a cruel thing, and I have not been the same since the day you left. You were so young, not yet twenty, and I was too stubborn to listen when you pleaded for a chance to stay. I told you I had a duty to this lighthouse, that the sea was my life, and that you would only be a burden. I was wrong, of course. I was always wrong.

I have not spoken to you in all this time, and I have not written. But tonight, as the storm gathers in the east, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. The wind howls like a beast, and the waves grow wilder by the moment. I have been here for thirty years, and I have seen many storms, but this one feels different. It feels like the end of something. Perhaps the end of me.

I have always believed that the lighthouse is a beacon for others, but tonight, I fear it may be the last light I will ever see. The lantern is failing, and the oil is low. I have been trying to

**Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these old stone walls. The sea rages, the sky is black as pitch, and the lantern atop this tower flickers like a dying ember. I've spent a lifetime watching the tides and the stars, but tonight, I fear the heavens themselves may not be enough to keep me from the dark.

Five long years have passed since the last time I laid eyes on you. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of your name lingering in the corners of my mind like a ghost I cannot shake. I've tried to forget, to move on, to let the waves carry my regrets away. But the sea has never been kind to those who carry burdens too heavy to bear.

I've always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be a man of steady resolve, a sentinel against the chaos of the world. But tonight, I find myself unable to stand firm. The storm is not just a tempest of wind and water—it is a reckoning. And I have no doubt that when the dawn breaks, I may not be here to greet it.

You were my light, Eliza. My guiding star in the darkest of nights. I remember the days we spent by the sea, the way you laughed as the waves crashed against the rocks, the way your eyes would light up when you saw the beacon shine. You were everything I thought I had lost when your

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with a heart heavy with years of silence, and a mind clouded by the storm that gathers beyond the cliffs. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though the miles between us have grown vast, it is the distance of our hearts that feels most unbearable.

I have kept this lighthouse for many years, standing firm against the tempests that rage upon the sea. Yet tonight, the winds howl with a ferocity I have never known, and I fear they may take more than just the light from this tower. I do not know if I shall see another dawn, and if I do not, I would have you know that I have never ceased to think of you.

You were but a child when you left, and though I tried to understand, I could not fathom why you would choose to walk away from the only family you had left. I have tried to forgive, but the wounds of that time remain. I have not spoken to you because I did not know how to. Words, once so easy, have grown scarce in the silence between us.

I have watched the sea with you in my thoughts, and I have prayed for your return. But if this storm claims me, I hope you will not let the silence between us remain forever. I have no regrets in my life, only the longing for the daughter I once knew. If you can hear my voice, know that I am

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the storm that brews beyond the cliffs, and with hands that tremble not from the wind, but from the weight of words unspoken for five long years. Time has not softened the edges of our estrangement, nor has it mended the wounds we have left unsaid.

I have watched the sea rage with fury, as if it too knows the sorrow I carry. Tonight, the tempest is upon us, and I fear I may not see dawn. Yet I write, for I have not yet given up hope that you might read these words, and that they might reach you before the waves take me.

You were once my light, Eliza. A beacon in the darkness, guiding me through the longest nights. I remember the days when you would come to visit, with your mother, and we would sit by the fire, sharing stories of the world beyond these stones. You spoke of London, of books, of the theatre, of dreams. I listened, and I longed to be part of your world. But time and distance have pulled us apart, and I have not been the father you deserved.

I do not know what has changed, nor if there is still a place for me in your heart. But I would ask you this: if there is even a sliver of kindness left in you, let me know that I am still your father. Let me know that I have

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

**April 12th, 1889**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not be the message you expected. I have not written to you in five years, and I fear that time has done little to mend the wounds we both carry. You were young when you left—just twenty-one, with a heart full of dreams and a mind full of questions. I was too proud to listen, too stubborn to see that you were not the daughter I had imagined.

I have spent these years in this lighthouse, alone with the sea, the wind, and the silence. The light burns every night, as it has for thirty years, and the waves have always been a constant companion. But tonight is different. A storm is coming—one that I do not believe the lighthouse can withstand. I have seen the sky darken with a fury I have never known, and I do not think I will be able to keep it at bay. I have no one to call for help, no one to speak to, and no one to tell this story to. So I write it now, with trembling hands and a heart that aches for the one person who once believed in me.

I have not forgotten the day you left. I remember the way you stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes filled with something I could not name. You

**[A weathered envelope, the seal cracked, the wax melting in the damp air]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse. The sea roars in fury, and the sky is black as midnight, lit only by the flickering glow of the lamp. I’ve been watching the storm for hours, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. I’ve not heard your voice, nor seen your face, and yet I feel your absence in every gust of wind and every crashing wave. I’ve tried to write to you, but the words always seemed to fall short of what I wanted to say. Perhaps it was my pride, or perhaps it was the weight of the sea pressing down on my soul.

You were a spirited girl, full of fire and defiance. You never asked for much, but when you did, you expected it. I remember the day you left—your eyes were filled with anger, your voice with tears. You told me I was a fool for choosing duty over family. And I was. I still am.

But I did what I thought was right. I was born to this lighthouse, Eliza. The sea called to me, and I answered. I have no home but these rocks, no family but the wind and the

**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**

*To My Dearest Eleanor,*

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee at my back. The sea rages, the skies darken, and I fear that tonight may be my last. Yet, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to put pen to paper, to speak words that I have long kept locked away within me.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, I have done my best to forget you. But the sea has a way of reminding us of things we'd rather not remember. It whispers your name in the crash of the waves, and I find myself wondering if you are still out there, somewhere, waiting for a chance to return.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and I have learned that the sea is a fickle mistress. It has taken many lives, and tonight, I fear it may take mine. But even as I write these words, I cannot help but think of you. I have never been the kind of man to express my feelings, but in this moment, I must try.

You were my greatest joy, my greatest sorrow. I have watched you grow into a woman of strength and spirit, and I have watched you turn your back on your father. I have tried to forgive you, but I have not been able to forget the pain of your departure. I have been told that you are married

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow, for the winds howl like the wails of the damned and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very land itself. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet the silence between us has grown heavier with each passing day. I have not heard from you, nor have I dared to write, for what words could I offer that would not sting like the salt in my eyes? I have tried to forget, but the memory of your last words—your cold, unyielding tone—has haunted me like the ghosts of those who once called this place home.

You were young then, and perhaps too full of dreams to see the truth of the world. I told you the truth, as any father must. That this life is not for the faint of heart. That the sea does not forgive, and that the light must burn, even when the world is dark. I told you I would not let the light go out, not while I still drew breath. And so I have stood here, night after night, through the years, alone with the wind and the waves, and the silence of a daughter who no longer calls me father.

I do not know if you will ever read this. Perhaps the storm will claim me before the letter reaches you. Perhaps it will be found,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not only from the cold wind that howls through the stone walls of this lighthouse, but from the weight of the years that have passed between us. You were twelve when I last saw you, and now the sea has swallowed five of them, and I fear it may soon claim me as well.

The storm is upon us, Eliza. The sky is a bruise of black, the waves rise like the wrath of some ancient god, and the lighthouse beacon—my duty, my life—flickers with a feeble light. I have spent my days watching the horizon, guiding the ships that pass, but tonight, I cannot see the shore. I can only see the dark, and the fear that it may swallow me whole.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know that I have no right to write to you now. You left, and I did not chase you. I told myself that I was protecting you, that the world was too cruel, too dangerous, and that the sea was the only place I could be safe. But I was wrong, Eliza. I was not safe. I was alone.

I have not forgotten the way you used to sit by the fire, your fingers curling around the edges of the pages of your books, your eyes alight with the wonder of the world. You were the only thing that made this life

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write these words with trembling hands, the wind howling like the devil himself outside this old stone tower. The sea, that ever-faithful companion, has turned against me, and I fear I may not see another dawn. But I must write, for if I do not, I shall be left with the weight of silence.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not brought you back. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, since that day you stormed out, your eyes like fire and your heart like stone. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried with all the love I possess, to reach out to you, but you have always been too far away.

You were but a child when you left, and now you are a woman of the world, I suppose. I have heard from the village that you are married, and that you have a child of your own. I do not know what to say of that. Perhaps it is better that I do not know. You have always been a puzzle, and I have always been the one trying to solve it.

The storm is upon us now, and I fear for my life. The waves have grown monstrous, crashing against the cliffs with a fury that I have not seen since the day the lighthouse was built. I have lit the lamp many times, as I always do, but tonight, I fear it may not be enough.

**April 12th, 1897**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. I write to you from the cliffs of Whitmore Point, where the sea howls like a wounded beast and the wind bites like a knife. The storm is upon us, and I pray the heavens will grant me the strength to see this through. I have spent a lifetime watching the waves, Eliza, and now, in my twilight years, I find myself watching for something else—something I once believed I would never see again.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of hearts that have grown colder with each passing day. I have tried to forgive you, as any father must, but I cannot forget the day you walked out of our home with your mother, your eyes filled with more than just sorrow—they were filled with something else. I have wondered if it was anger, or fear, or perhaps simply the weight of the world pressing too heavily upon your young shoulders.

I have not spoken to you in five years, but I have not forgotten. I have kept your room as it was, the way you left it, the books you once read, the photographs of you as a child. I have told myself that one day, perhaps, you might return. But I fear the day may never come. I have heard whispers that you have taken

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow. Five long years have passed since the last time I saw your face, and I fear that this may be the last letter I shall ever send. The storm that howls at the edge of the horizon is not merely a tempest of wind and rain—it is a tempest within me, one that has long been brewing and now threatens to consume all that I hold dear.

You were always a spirited child, full of questions and dreams, yet it was your father’s silence that became the greatest chasm between us. I regret the words I spoke, the times I turned away, and the love I let fester into bitterness. I have no excuse for my behavior, for I was a fool to let pride and fear cloud my judgment. You were my light, my guiding star, and I squandered the chance to be there for you.

I have watched the sea for years, its moods as fickle as a child’s temper, and I have learned to read its signs. Tonight, the sky darkens with unnatural fury. The waves grow restless, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for every storm, and I pray that it will guide the ships that brave these waters. But I fear that this time, the sea will not be so merciful.

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I write to you from the cold, salt-laden air of this desolate lighthouse, where the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with a fury that makes my bones tremble. It has been five years since I last saw you, and five years since you left me, with words as sharp as the sea spray that lashes the cliffs. I have not forgotten the day you walked out, your face pale, your eyes filled with something that felt like a storm of its own. I pray the storm tonight will be the last one I endure.

You were always too quick to judge, Clara. Too quick to believe the world was cruel and that I, your father, was the villain in your story. I was not the man you thought I was. I did not abandon you. I tried to keep the light burning, both in this tower and in the heart of our family. But the world is cruel, and I was not strong enough to stand against it.

I have been alone here for years, with only the sea and the wind for company. The lighthouse is more than a place—it is a part of me. I have tended the flame, kept the light alive, and watched the waves crash against the rocks with a steady hand. I have done what I could to protect this place, and in doing so, I have forgotten the warmth of your presence.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old stone keep. The sea roars as if it too has heard of your name, and I fear the storm that brews on the horizon may not be the only thing to claim me tonight. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five long years, and I have not forgotten you.

I have watched the sun rise and set a thousand times since you left, each dawn a reminder of the world you once shared with me. I have tended the light, kept the lantern burning, and listened to the waves, but never once have I dared to call out to you. I feared that if I did, you might not answer, and the silence would only deepen the wound that time has not healed.

You were a fire, Eliza, and I was the keeper of the flame. You left, and I remained, clinging to the hope that one day you would return. But the years have worn me down, and the salt of the sea has crept into my bones. I have grown old, my hands no longer steady, my eyes no longer sharp. Yet I still stand here, waiting, hoping.

I have not forgotten the days we shared—your laughter, your dreams, the way you would climb the spiral stairs to see the world from above. You were my light, and I was your keeper. I

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heavy heart and trembling hands, for the storm that looms on the horizon has brought with it not only the threat of ruin, but the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since our last words, and though the sea has not ceased its ceaseless motion, the silence between us has grown ever more deafening.

I have kept this lighthouse for many years, and though I have always been a man of steadfast duty, I have not been able to keep the same steadfastness in our bond. I have thought of you often, my dear, and I have wondered what path you have taken, what world you now inhabit. I have heard from the village that you have found your own way, and I suppose I must be content with that, though it pains me to think of you so far from the only home you ever knew.

Tonight, the skies darken with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The winds howl like the very souls of the lost, and the waves rise with a vengeance that makes my bones ache. I have seen the signs, and I fear that this storm may not pass without cost. I have lived my life in service to the sea, and I have always believed that it would take more than a tempest to claim me.

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since our last exchange, and though the sea has not forgotten its tides, I fear I have forgotten how to speak to you without fear of your silence.

The wind howls like a beast upon the cliffs, and the sea roars as if in mourning. I have kept this lighthouse lit for many a storm, but tonight feels different—more cruel, more relentless. The waves crash against the rocks with a fury I have not seen in decades. I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

I have often wondered if you still remember the days when we walked the shore together, your laughter like the sound of the waves. I have thought of your mother often, and how she would have loved to see you grow. But you have not written, and I have not dared to reach out. I feared your anger, your disappointment. And so, I let time pass.

If I do not survive this night, know that I have never stopped loving you. I have watched the stars for years, and in their light, I have seen your face. I have dreamed of you, and I have prayed for you. I hope, in some way, you are safe and well.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since the last time I saw your face, and yet the memory of your laughter still lingers like the scent of sea salt in the air before a storm. Tonight, the sky is dark with clouds that promise ruin, and I find myself wondering if I will live to see the dawn. But if I do, I hope you will read this letter, and if I don't, I hope you will forgive me.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not because I do not care, but because I feared the words would not be enough. I feared they would be too late. I have spent these years watching the sea from this lonely tower, lighting the beacon each night, and hoping that one day, you would return. But you did not. You left, and I stayed.

I do not know what you think of me. I do not know if you remember the last time we spoke. I only know that I have carried the silence like a stone in my chest, and it has grown heavier with every passing year. I have tried to be the father you needed, but I failed. I have tried to be the man you deserved, but I faltered. And now, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the waves crash against the rocks below, I am left with only regret.

**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a heavy heart, for the ink upon this page feels as though it might drown me, just as the storm that now brews upon the horizon threatens to do. Five years have passed since our last exchange, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. Yet here I am, on this bleak and wind-swept night, with the sea howling like a beast and the light of the lighthouse flickering like a dying star.

You were once my light, my hope, my only child. But time and distance have cast a shadow over our bond, and I fear that even now, with the world crumbling around us, I may not be able to reach you. I have spent these years in solitude, the lighthouse my only companion, the sea my only friend. And yet, as I look out at the tempest gathering, I am reminded that even the most steadfast light may be extinguished.

I have heard the tales of your life in the city, of your choices, of the path you have taken. I do not know if you have found peace or if you still carry the weight of our separation. But I write to you now, not as a father who has been wronged, but as a man who has come to understand that love is not always a matter of right or wrong, but of

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast unleashed upon the cliffs, and the sea roiling as though it too weeps. The lighthouse stands firm, though I fear it may not stand much longer. I have been here for many years, but never have I felt so alone.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I wonder if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if it has faded like the light that once guided your way home. I have tried, in silence, to write to you, but the words always seem to fall short of what I wish to say. I have no one to read them to, and so I must leave them to the wind, as I once left you.

I have watched the waves crash against these rocks, and I have wondered if you are watching them from afar, as I once did from the window of our home.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better spirits than I have found myself. It has been five long years since the last time we spoke, and I have been meaning to write to you, to set things right, to make amends. But words have always been difficult between us, and I fear that even now, with the storm gathering on the horizon, I may not be able to say all that I need to say.

You were right, of course. I was too rigid, too stubborn, too wrapped up in the duties of this lighthouse to see the world through your eyes. I was too busy ensuring the safe passage of ships and the light of the beacon never failed, to see that you were growing up, changing, becoming someone I no longer recognized. I was too afraid to reach out, to ask for forgiveness, to understand that you were not the child I had raised, but a woman with her own thoughts, her own dreams, and her own heart.

I still remember the night you left. The wind howled like a wounded beast, the sea roared with fury, and I stood at the top of the tower, watching the light flicker and dance upon the waves. I thought I was protecting you, ensuring your safety, but in truth, I was keeping you from the world. You were not meant to be trapped in this tower, not meant to be left behind while I tended to the lamp and the lanterns.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wolves that once prowled the moors where we used to walk. The sea is restless tonight, and the light from the tower flickers like a dying ember. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet here I am, pen in hand, heart heavy with words I have long kept buried.

I know you think I abandoned you. That I chose the sea over the child you were. But I never left you. I have been here, night after night, keeping the light burning, hoping that one day, you would come back. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks, and I have whispered your name into the wind, hoping it would carry to you. I have dreamed of you, Eleanor. I have dreamed of the day you would return.

I do not know if I will survive this storm. The waves have grown taller, the wind more furious. The lighthouse, though strong, is not invincible. I have lived here for twenty years, and I have seen many storms. But this one feels different. It feels like the end.

If I do not make it through, I leave you this letter. Do not read it until the light is gone. Do not read it until the storm has passed. Let the sea decide when you are ready to hear the truth.

I never stopped loving you, Eleanor. I never stopped watching for you

**[The Lighthouse, 1894]**

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old tower. The sea has grown restless, and the sky is dark as a funeral shroud. I've been watching the waves for hours, and I can feel the storm coming—like a beast awakening from a long slumber. I fear I may not see morning.

Five years have passed since the last time I saw you, and I have not spoken to you since. I know you believe I have abandoned you, that I have chosen this lonely life over the warmth of your home, your laughter, your presence. But I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. Every night, I sit by the light and think of you. I speak your name to the wind, hoping it will carry it to you.

You were only ten when I left. I had promised your mother I would return, but the sea was unkind, and I was called away. I thought I would come back soon. I thought I would bring you with me. But the world is not as kind as I hoped, and I have not been able to return. I have been a fool, Eleanor. I have been a fool to think I could leave this place and still find my way back.
